atlasSubtitle: Interactive local explorer for bacteria, cells, organelles

cells.label: Plant Cell
cells.summary: Plant cell with rigid cell wall, large central vacuole, and chloroplasts.
cells.funFact: Central vacuole can occupy up to 80 % of total cell volume and is the main driver of turgor pressure.
facts: Chloroplasts photosynthesise using stacked thylakoid grana.
Central vacuole can occupy up to 80% of cell volume.
Cell wall is mainly cellulose microfibrils.
Plasmodesmata connect neighbouring cells.

components.label: Cell wall
components.fact: Cellulose fibrils give plant cell walls roughly the tensile strength of steel by mass.

components.label: Plasma membrane
components.fact: Membrane proteins regulate ion flow and signalling.

components.label: Central vacuole
components.fact: Stores water, ions, sugars, pigments, and waste.

components.label: Chloroplasts
components.fact: Have their own circular DNA from cyanobacterial endosymbiosis.

components.label: Nucleus
components.fact: Contains nucleolus where ribosomes are assembled.

components.label: Mitochondria
components.fact: Plants ALSO have mitochondria, not just chloroplasts.

components.label: Ribosomes
components.fact: Plant cytoplasmic ribosomes are 80S, plastid ribosomes are 70S.

cad.prompt: Create a stylised plant cell: cubic 2.0 mm rigid cell wall with chamfered corners, inner spherical membrane 1.7 mm, central spherical vacuole 1.2 mm, 6 lens-shaped chloroplasts 0.4 mm major radius placed between wall and vacuole, one 0.45 mm nucleus, 4 mitochondria capsules. Separate named parts. Export STEP and GLB.

cells.label: Animal Cell
cells.summary: Generic animal eukaryotic cell with membrane, nucleus, ER, Golgi, mitochondria, ribosomes.
cells.funFact: A single human cell contains roughly 2 m of DNA folded into a 6 µm nucleus.
facts: Nucleus encloses DNA inside a double membrane with nuclear pores.
Mitochondria are the ATP powerhouses with their own circular genome.
Endoplasmic reticulum folds proteins and synthesises lipids.
Golgi apparatus packages and ships proteins in vesicles.

components.label: Plasma membrane
components.fact: Each cell has roughly 100 million lipid molecules in its membrane.

components.label: Nucleus
components.fact: Nuclear pores let mRNA out and ribosomal proteins in selectively.

components.label: Nucleolus
components.fact: Disassembles during mitosis and reassembles afterwards.

components.label: Mitochondria
components.fact: You inherit your mitochondria entirely from your mother.

components.label: Endoplasmic reticulum
components.fact: Rough ER is studded with ribosomes; smooth ER builds lipids.

components.label: Golgi apparatus
components.fact: Adds sugar tags to proteins (glycosylation).

components.label: Ribosomes
components.fact: Each cell has millions of ribosomes; ~10 million in active tissue.

components.label: Cytoskeleton
components.fact: Motor proteins (kinesin, dynein) walk along microtubule tracks.

cad.prompt: Build a stylised animal cell: 14 mm outer spherical membrane, 5 mm nucleus with nucleolus, 8 mitochondria ellipsoids, ER as a twisted tube network, 3 stacked Golgi discs, 40 ribosome microspheres on the ER. Export STEP and GLB.

cells.label: Neuron
cells.size: soma 20 µm, axon up to 1 m
cells.summary: Excitable cell with dendrites, a single axon, myelin sheath, and synapse terminals.
cells.funFact: The longest neurons in your body run from the spinal cord to your toes — over 1 m, but still one continuous cell.
facts: Action potential travels ~100 m/s on myelinated axons.
A single neuron can form thousands of synaptic contacts.
Myelin is produced by Schwann cells (PNS) or oligodendrocytes (CNS).
Ion channels gate Na+ and K+ to propagate depolarisation.

components.label: Soma
components.fact: Houses the nucleus and most organelles.

components.label: Dendrites
components.fact: Receive thousands of synaptic inputs simultaneously.

components.label: Axon
components.fact: Single output cable — branches at the terminal end.

components.label: Myelin sheath
components.fact: Allows action potentials to jump between Nodes of Ranvier.

components.label: Nodes of Ranvier
components.fact: Dense Na+ channel concentration regenerates the action potential.

components.label: Synaptic terminals
components.fact: Release neurotransmitters across a 20 nm synaptic cleft.

cad.prompt: Create a stylised neuron with soma sphere, branching dendrites, long axon with myelin segments, and terminal boutons.

cells.label: Epithelial Cell
cells.summary: Polarised cuboidal/columnar cell with apical microvilli and tight junctions to neighbours.
cells.funFact: Microvilli on intestinal epithelial cells multiply the absorbing surface of your gut about 30 ×.
facts: Polarised: apical (top) microvilli vs basal (bottom) basement membrane.
Tight junctions seal the gaps between adjacent cells.
Renew rapidly — gut epithelium turns over every 4-5 days.
Major barrier and absorption surface in skin, gut, lung, kidney.

components.label: Plasma membrane
components.fact: Apical and basolateral domains have different proteins.

components.label: Microvilli
components.fact: Each microvillus is supported by an actin filament bundle.

components.label: Tight junctions
components.fact: Made of claudins and occludins; selective paracellular barrier.

components.label: Basement membrane
components.fact: Sheet of collagen IV + laminin holding the epithelium to underlying tissue.

components.label: Nucleus
components.fact: Usually located near the basal end in columnar cells.

components.label: Mitochondria
components.fact: Heavily concentrated near the basal membrane in transport-active cells.

cad.prompt: Stylised epithelial cell: cuboidal body 1.6 mm tall, microvilli on apical face, basement membrane plate underneath, tight-junction ring near top, basal nucleus.

cells.label: Stem Cell
cells.summary: Undifferentiated cell with self-renewal and pluripotent / multipotent potential.
cells.funFact: Pluripotent stem cells can give rise to all 200+ cell types in the body.
facts: Self-renew via symmetric division and produce committed daughter cells.
Express markers like OCT4, SOX2, NANOG (pluripotent) or CD34, CD133 (adult).
Bone marrow stem cells produce ~10¹¹ blood cells per day.
Niche signals tightly control whether they self-renew or differentiate.

components.label: Plasma membrane
components.fact: Carries surface markers used to identify stem cell type.

components.label: Large nucleus
components.fact: Stem cells have proportionally huge nuclei vs cytoplasm.

components.label: Open chromatin
components.fact: Loose chromatin keeps developmental genes accessible.

components.label: Surface markers
components.fact: Used in flow cytometry to sort stem cells from blood/marrow.

components.label: Daughter buds
components.fact: Asymmetric division can produce one stem + one differentiated daughter.

components.label: Mitochondria
components.fact: Pluripotent cells favour glycolysis over oxidative phosphorylation.

cad.prompt: Stylised pluripotent stem cell: 1.4 mm sphere, large 0.85 mm nucleus, scattered chromatin patches, surface marker spikes, 4 small daughter buds at the equator.

cells.label: Muscle Cell
cells.summary: Long multinucleated skeletal myocyte packed with myofibrils and striated sarcomeres.
cells.funFact: Skeletal muscle fibres can be over 30 cm long and contain hundreds of nuclei.
facts: Multinucleated — many nuclei sit just under the sarcolemma.
Sarcomere is the contractile unit; striations come from aligned sarcomeres.
Myofibrils are bundles of actin + myosin filaments.
T-tubules carry action potentials deep into the fibre to trigger Ca²⁺ release.

components.label: Sarcolemma
components.fact: Has T-tubule invaginations that propagate excitation inward.

components.label: Myofibrils
components.fact: Hundreds run end-to-end through each fibre.

components.label: Sarcomere bands
components.fact: Contraction shortens each sarcomere by ~30 %.

components.label: Peripheral nuclei
components.fact: Nuclei sit beneath the sarcolemma so myofibrils have room in the centre.

components.label: Mitochondria
components.fact: Densely packed between myofibrils — endurance fibres have more.

cad.prompt: Stylised skeletal muscle cell: capsule 3.6 mm long × 0.42 mm radius, parallel myofibril cylinders, sarcomere band rings, peripheral oblong nuclei, scattered mitochondria between fibrils.

cells.label: T-cell
cells.summary: Adaptive immune cell with T-cell receptors, actively patrolling for antigens.
cells.funFact: Naive T-cells visit nearly every lymph node in the body roughly once a day.
facts: TCR recognises peptide presented by MHC on target cells.
CD8+ cytotoxic T-cells release perforin + granzymes.
Naive T-cells circulate through lymph nodes continuously.
Memory T-cells give durable adaptive immunity.

components.label: Plasma membrane
components.fact: Forms an immunological synapse on antigen contact.

components.label: T-cell receptors
components.fact: Each T-cell expresses a single TCR specificity.

components.label: Large nucleus
components.fact: Takes up most of the small T-cell volume.

components.label: Mitochondria
components.fact: Reposition toward the synapse during target killing.

components.label: Cytotoxic granules
components.fact: Polarise toward the target cell, then release lethal cargo.

cad.prompt: Stylised T-cell: 1.0 mm sphere, large nucleus, surface TCR microspikes, internal cytotoxic granules.

cells.label: E. coli
cells.summary: Classic rod-shaped Gram-negative bacterium with peritrichous flagella.
cells.funFact: An E. coli with flagella can swim 20 body lengths per second — relatively faster than a cheetah.
facts: Genome is a single circular chromosome (~4.6 Mb).
Flagella rotate like propellers at ~300 Hz.
Divides every ~20 minutes in rich medium.
Has a pilus (sex pilus) for plasmid conjugation.

components.label: Cell envelope
components.fact: Gram-negatives have an extra outer LPS membrane.

components.label: Nucleoid
components.fact: Not enclosed by a membrane — just a tightly supercoiled region.

components.label: 70S ribosomes
components.fact: Smaller than eukaryotic 80S — antibiotics target this difference.

components.label: Flagella
components.fact: Driven by a proton-motive force rotor — a true biological motor.

components.label: Pili
components.fact: Conjugative pili transfer plasmids between bacteria.

cad.prompt: Stylised E. coli: capsule body, helical flagella, straight pili, twisted nucleoid loop.

cells.label: SARS-CoV-2
cells.summary: Enveloped positive-sense RNA virus with spike, envelope, membrane, and nucleocapsid proteins.
cells.funFact: About 50 SARS-CoV-2 viruses fit across the diameter of a single human red blood cell.
facts: Spike protein binds ACE2 receptor on host cells.
Genome ~30 kb positive-sense single-stranded RNA.
Envelope derived from host endoplasmic reticulum.
Mutations in spike drive variant emergence.

components.label: Lipid envelope
components.fact: Detergent-sensitive — soap disrupts it easily.

components.label: Spike trimers
components.fact: Each spike is a trimer; mutations here drive variants.

components.label: M + E proteins
components.fact: Most abundant structural protein is M, organising the envelope.

components.label: RNA + N protein
components.fact: N protein coats the RNA into a long flexible nucleocapsid.

lifecycle.label: Attachment
lifecycle.narration: Spike protein binds ACE2 receptor on the host cell surface.

lifecycle.label: Entry
lifecycle.narration: TMPRSS2 cleaves spike; viral envelope fuses with host membrane and releases RNA.

lifecycle.label: Replication
lifecycle.narration: Host ribosomes translate viral RNA into RdRp + structural proteins; RNA is copied.

lifecycle.label: Assembly
lifecycle.narration: Genome + N protein + M + E proteins assemble at ERGIC; spike is glycosylated.

lifecycle.label: Release
lifecycle.narration: New virions bud through the host membrane and exit by exocytosis.

cad.prompt: Stylised SARS-CoV-2: icosphere envelope, 32 spike trimers, helical RNA inside.

cells.label: Bacteriophage T4
cells.summary: Iconic tailed dsDNA virus that lands on bacteria like a lunar lander and injects its DNA.
cells.funFact: T4 phage looks more like a 1960s spacecraft than anything else in biology — with a head, neck, contractile sheath, baseplate, and six articulated landing legs.
facts: Genome ~169 kb double-stranded DNA inside the icosahedral head.
Tail sheath contracts on landing, driving the inner tube into the bacterium.
Six tail fibers recognise lipopolysaccharide on E. coli outer membrane.
Each infection cycle releases ~100-200 progeny phages in 25-30 minutes.

components.label: Head capsid
components.fact: Pressurised with DNA at ~6 MPa — like a bottled spring.

components.label: Collar
components.fact: Contains the portal protein DNA passes through during packaging.

components.label: Tail sheath
components.fact: Contracts to half its length, generating 50 piconewtons of force.

components.label: Tail tube
components.fact: Acts as a syringe needle through the bacterial wall.

components.label: Base plate
components.fact: Senses host cell receptors, triggers tail contraction.

components.label: Tail fibers
components.fact: Articulated like spider legs; bind LPS on E. coli surface.

components.label: dsDNA genome
components.fact: Linear genome circularises and integrates after injection.

lifecycle.label: Attachment
lifecycle.narration: Tail fibers recognise LPS on the bacterial outer membrane and lock on.

lifecycle.label: Injection
lifecycle.narration: Sheath contracts, driving the tail tube through the wall; DNA is injected.

lifecycle.label: Replication
lifecycle.narration: Phage DNA hijacks bacterial machinery to make new genomes + structural proteins.

lifecycle.label: Assembly
lifecycle.narration: Heads, tails, fibers self-assemble in the host cytoplasm; DNA is packaged into heads.

lifecycle.label: Lysis
lifecycle.narration: Phage lysozyme bursts the bacterium, releasing ~150 progeny phages.

cad.prompt: T4 bacteriophage: icosahedral head, contractile tail sheath, base plate, six articulated tail fibers, internal helical dsDNA.

cells.label: HIV virion
cells.summary: Retrovirus with conical capsid, two RNA copies, and reverse transcriptase that integrates into host DNA.
cells.funFact: HIV's signature cone-shaped capsid is built from ~250 hexamers + 12 pentamers — a beautifully irregular polyhedron.
facts: Carries two copies of single-stranded RNA + reverse transcriptase + integrase.
Reverse transcriptase converts RNA into dsDNA, which integrates into the host genome.
Spikes are gp120 (binds CD4 + CCR5/CXCR4) on top of gp41 stalks.
After integration, the provirus can stay latent for years.

components.label: Lipid envelope
components.fact: Studded with host MHC and adhesion proteins as well as viral spikes.

components.label: gp120 / gp41 spikes
components.fact: gp120 binds CD4; gp41 mediates membrane fusion.

components.label: Conical capsid (p24)
components.fact: The cone is mathematically a fullerene with 12 pentamers — 7 at one end, 5 at the other.

components.label: ssRNA + RT + integrase
components.fact: RT makes ≈1 mutation per genome per replication — this is why HIV evolves so fast.

lifecycle.label: Attachment
lifecycle.narration: gp120 binds CD4 on T-helper cells, then engages CCR5 or CXCR4 co-receptor.

lifecycle.label: Fusion + entry
lifecycle.narration: gp41 conformational change pulls the viral and host membranes together; capsid enters cytoplasm.

lifecycle.label: Reverse transcription
lifecycle.narration: RT converts viral RNA into dsDNA inside the capsid, which transports through the nuclear pore.

lifecycle.label: Integration
lifecycle.narration: Integrase inserts dsDNA into the host genome — the cell is now permanently infected.

lifecycle.label: Assembly + budding
lifecycle.narration: New virions assemble at the plasma membrane and bud off; protease then matures the conical capsid.

cad.prompt: HIV virion: spherical envelope with gp120/gp41 spike trimers, internal conical capsid, two RNA strands with RT clusters.

cells.label: Influenza A
cells.summary: Segmented negative-sense RNA virus with two spike types: HA (hemagglutinin) and NA (neuraminidase).
cells.funFact: Influenza's genome is split into 8 separate RNA segments — when two strains co-infect a host they can shuffle segments and create a pandemic strain.
facts: HA binds sialic acid receptors and triggers membrane fusion in endosomes.
NA cleaves sialic acid so progeny virions can detach from the host.
Antigenic drift = mutations in HA/NA; antigenic shift = whole-segment reassortment.
H1N1, H3N2, H5N1 etc. name the HA + NA subtypes.

components.label: Lipid envelope
components.fact: Pleomorphic — spherical and filamentous forms both exist.

components.label: Hemagglutinin (HA)
components.fact: Outnumbers NA roughly 4:1 on the surface.

components.label: Neuraminidase (NA)
components.fact: Target of oseltamivir (Tamiflu) and zanamivir.

components.label: 8 RNA segments + N
components.fact: Reassortment of these 8 segments drives pandemic emergence.

lifecycle.label: Attachment
lifecycle.narration: HA binds sialic acid on respiratory epithelial cells.

lifecycle.label: Endocytosis
lifecycle.narration: Virion is engulfed; low-pH endosome triggers HA conformational change → fusion.

lifecycle.label: Replication (nucleus)
lifecycle.narration: Unusually for an RNA virus, influenza replicates inside the nucleus using viral RdRp.

lifecycle.label: Assembly
lifecycle.narration: All 8 segments + structural proteins traffic to the plasma membrane and pack into new virions.

lifecycle.label: Release
lifecycle.narration: NA cleaves sialic acid so progeny virions detach and spread.

cad.prompt: Influenza A virion: spherical envelope with HA trimer clubs and NA tetramer mushrooms; 8 internal RNA segments.

cells.label: Adenovirus
cells.summary: Non-enveloped icosahedral dsDNA virus with characteristic fiber projections at each vertex — used as gene-therapy vector.
cells.funFact: Adenoviruses inspired modern gene-therapy and vaccine vectors (e.g. ChAdOx1 in the AstraZeneca COVID-19 vaccine).
facts: Capsid has 252 capsomers: 240 hexons (faces) + 12 pentons (vertices).
Each penton has one fiber projection that ends in a knob.
dsDNA genome ~36 kb, no envelope, very stable.
Causes respiratory, eye, and GI infections; engineered for gene delivery.

components.label: Icosahedral capsid
components.fact: Made from hexon (240) + penton (12) proteins.

components.label: Fiber projections
components.fact: Knob at the tip determines tissue tropism.

components.label: dsDNA core
components.fact: Linear, with terminal protein covalently attached to each end.

lifecycle.label: Attachment
lifecycle.narration: Fiber knob binds CAR or CD46 on host cells.

lifecycle.label: Endocytosis
lifecycle.narration: Penton base engages integrins; virus is internalised in clathrin-coated vesicles.

lifecycle.label: Endosomal escape
lifecycle.narration: Capsid disassembles in the endosome, viral DNA is delivered to nuclear pore.

lifecycle.label: Replication
lifecycle.narration: Viral DNA polymerase replicates dsDNA inside the nucleus using viral terminal protein primer.

lifecycle.label: Release
lifecycle.narration: New virions accumulate, eventually lysing the host cell to spread.

cad.prompt: Adenovirus: icosahedral capsid with 12 fiber projections (each ending in a knob) at the vertices, internal dsDNA core.

cells.label: Red Blood Cell
cells.summary: Anucleate biconcave-disc cell packed with hemoglobin — the dedicated oxygen courier.
cells.funFact: You make about 2 million new red blood cells every second, and each one carries roughly 270 million hemoglobin molecules.
facts: Mature RBCs eject their nucleus during erythropoiesis to maximise hemoglobin space.
Biconcave disc shape gives high surface area + flexibility to squeeze through capillaries.
Average lifespan ~120 days before being recycled in the spleen and liver.
Surface antigens (ABO, Rh) define blood groups for transfusion compatibility.

components.label: Plasma membrane
components.fact: Spectrin cytoskeleton lets the cell deform through 3 µm capillaries.

components.label: Hemoglobin
components.fact: Each Hb tetramer can carry 4 O₂ molecules cooperatively.

components.label: Surface antigens
components.fact: ABO + Rh are the most clinically important; ~30 systems exist.

cad.prompt: Biconcave disc red blood cell with central depression on both sides, surface antigen pattern, internal hemoglobin grain.

cells.label: Platelet
cells.summary: Anucleate cell fragment of megakaryocyte origin. Initiates clotting at wound sites.
cells.funFact: A single megakaryocyte can shed 1,000-3,000 platelets in its lifetime, and your body makes ~10^11 platelets every day.
facts: Biconvex disc shape; becomes star-like when activated.
No nucleus — just a cytoskeleton + granule fragment of megakaryocyte cytoplasm.
Alpha-granules carry clotting factors; dense-granules carry ADP/Ca²⁺.
Lifespan ~7-10 days in circulation.

components.label: Plasma membrane
components.fact: Sheds microparticles during activation.

components.label: Alpha + dense granules
components.fact: Release ADP + serotonin to recruit more platelets.

components.label: Marginal microtubule band
components.fact: Maintains the resting disc shape; rearranges on activation.

cad.prompt: Small biconvex discoid platelet with marginal microtubule ring and scattered alpha + dense granules.

cells.label: B-cell
cells.summary: Antibody-producing lymphocyte that recognises intact antigens through its B-cell receptor (BCR).
cells.funFact: Each B-cell has about 100,000 identical BCRs on its surface, all with a single unique antigen specificity.
facts: BCR is a membrane-bound antibody (Y-shaped immunoglobulin).
Upon activation, differentiates into plasma cells that secrete free antibodies.
Memory B-cells patrol for years after infection.
Class-switch recombination changes antibody isotype (IgM → IgG/IgA/IgE).

components.label: Plasma membrane
components.fact: Forms immunological synapse with antigen-presenting cells.

components.label: Large round nucleus
components.fact: Somatic hypermutation rewrites antibody genes here.

components.label: B-cell receptors (BCR)
components.fact: Membrane-bound antibody; surrounded by CD79a/b signal chains.

components.label: Mitochondria
components.fact: Ramped up when cells become antibody-secreting plasma cells.

cad.prompt: Lymphocyte with smooth membrane, large round nucleus, ~50 Y-shaped BCR receptors on surface, few mitochondria.

cells.label: Macrophage
cells.summary: Large tissue-resident phagocyte that engulfs pathogens and debris and presents antigens to T-cells.
cells.funFact: A single macrophage can engulf over 100 bacteria in its lifetime and lives for months to years in tissue.
facts: Derived from blood monocytes that settle into tissues.
Engulfs pathogens by phagocytosis → phagosome → phagolysosome.
Presents antigens on MHC class II to CD4+ helper T-cells.
Tissue-specific names: Kupffer cells (liver), microglia (brain), alveolar macrophages (lung), osteoclasts (bone).

components.label: Plasma membrane
components.fact: Reshapes dynamically during engulfment.

components.label: Kidney-bean nucleus
components.fact: Distinctive bean shape vs other leukocytes.

components.label: Pseudopodia
components.fact: Actin polymerisation drives extension around prey.

components.label: Phagosomes
components.fact: Fuse with lysosomes to digest contents.

components.label: Lysosomes
components.fact: Contain 50+ hydrolytic enzymes active at pH 4.5.

cad.prompt: Large irregular macrophage with lobed pseudopodia, kidney-bean nucleus, multiple phagosomes + lysosomes inside.

cells.label: Neutrophil
cells.summary: Most abundant white blood cell — first responder to bacterial infection. Characteristic multilobed nucleus.
cells.funFact: Neutrophils can release their own DNA as sticky 'NETs' (Neutrophil Extracellular Traps) to ensnare bacteria.
facts: ~60% of circulating leukocytes are neutrophils.
Polymorphonuclear (PMN) — nucleus has 3-5 connected lobes.
Kill by phagocytosis, degranulation, and NETosis.
Short half-life (~8 hours) in blood; bone marrow makes 10^11 per day.

components.label: Plasma membrane
components.fact: Has receptors for chemokines like IL-8 to find infection sites.

components.label: Multilobed nucleus
components.fact: Lobed shape helps squeeze through tight tissue gaps.

components.label: Granules
components.fact: Contain myeloperoxidase, defensins, elastase, lysozyme.

components.label: Pseudopodia
components.fact: Drive chemotactic migration up cytokine gradients.

cad.prompt: PMN neutrophil with 4-lobed nucleus connected by thin strands, ~50 granules, surface pseudopod bulges.

cells.label: Spermatozoon
cells.summary: Motile male gamete with condensed-DNA head, acrosome cap, mitochondrial midpiece, and a long flagellum.
cells.funFact: Mammalian sperm swim ~50-100 body lengths per second — and the entire human reproductive tract is only ~20 cm.
facts: Haploid (23 chromosomes in humans).
Acrosome cap releases enzymes that drill through the egg's zona pellucida.
Midpiece is packed with mitochondria that power the tail.
Flagellum beats in a helical wave at ~10 Hz.

components.label: Head
components.fact: Pointed shape streamlines swimming.

components.label: Acrosome cap
components.fact: Hyaluronidase + acrosin digest zona pellucida.

components.label: Condensed nucleus
components.fact: DNA packed with protamines (10x denser than histones).

components.label: Midpiece
components.fact: Helical spiral of mitochondria wrapping the axoneme.

components.label: Flagellum (tail)
components.fact: Axoneme is a 9+2 microtubule bundle driven by dynein.

cad.prompt: Human spermatozoon with pointed ovoid head + acrosome cap + midpiece mitochondrial spiral + long whip flagellum.

cells.label: Staphylococcus aureus
cells.summary: Gram-positive cocci that grow in grape-like clusters. Common skin commensal and major hospital pathogen.
cells.funFact: The name Staphylococcus comes from Greek staphylē = 'bunch of grapes' — because of the distinctive cluster morphology.
facts: Gram-positive — thick peptidoglycan wall, no outer membrane.
Divides in multiple planes, producing irregular grape-like clusters.
S. aureus produces coagulase, toxins (TSST-1, PVL), and enterotoxins.
MRSA = methicillin-resistant S. aureus — a major nosocomial threat.

components.label: Spherical cocci
components.fact: Divide in multiple planes to form clusters.

components.label: Peptidoglycan wall
components.fact: Target of penicillin and vancomycin.

components.label: Nucleoid
components.fact: Condensed region, no membrane.

components.label: 70S ribosomes
components.fact: Targets of aminoglycosides, tetracyclines, linezolid.

cad.prompt: Cluster of 9 spherical cocci in a grape-like arrangement, each with peptidoglycan wall ring, nucleoid, and ribosomes.

cells.label: Yeast
cells.summary: Unicellular fungus (Saccharomyces cerevisiae). Reproduces asexually by budding. Used in baking, brewing, and genetic research.
cells.funFact: Yeast was the first eukaryote to have its genome fully sequenced (1996) and remains a workhorse of molecular biology.
facts: Divides by budding — daughter cell grows off the mother and pinches off.
Has a rigid chitin + glucan cell wall.
Facultative anaerobe — can respire or ferment (makes ethanol + CO₂).
~6,000 genes vs ~20,000 in humans; many are orthologous.

components.label: Cell wall
components.fact: Gives yeast its rigid shape.

components.label: Plasma membrane
components.fact: Ergosterol target of antifungals (azoles, polyenes).

components.label: Nucleus
components.fact: Has a persistent nuclear envelope during closed mitosis.

components.label: Vacuole
components.fact: Analogous to plant vacuole / animal lysosome.

components.label: Mitochondria
components.fact: Few in fermenting cells, many in respiring cells.

components.label: Daughter bud
components.fact: Nuclear + organelle inheritance happens through a narrow neck.

cad.prompt: Spherical yeast cell (S. cerevisiae) with rigid cell wall, inner plasma membrane, nucleus, large vacuole, mitochondria, and a budding daughter cell.

cells.label: Paramecium
cells.summary: Slipper-shaped freshwater ciliate. Swims using thousands of coordinated cilia and has two nuclei.
cells.funFact: A single Paramecium beats roughly 10 million cilia × ~10 times per second — yet coordinates them into graceful helical swimming.
facts: Has two nuclei: a large macronucleus (somatic) and a small micronucleus (germline).
Feeds by sweeping bacteria into an oral groove → cytostome → food vacuoles.
Contractile vacuoles pump excess water out (freshwater osmoregulation).
Reproduces asexually by fission but can conjugate to exchange micronuclear DNA.

components.label: Pellicle
components.fact: Proteinaceous layer beneath the plasma membrane.

components.label: Cilia carpet
components.fact: Beat in coordinated metachronal waves across the surface.

components.label: Macronucleus
components.fact: Highly polyploid; drives all day-to-day gene expression.

components.label: Micronucleus
components.fact: Transmitted during conjugation; diploid.

components.label: Oral groove
components.fact: Cilia sweep food particles into the cytostome.

components.label: Contractile vacuole
components.fact: Expels water to counter osmotic influx in freshwater.

cad.prompt: Slipper-shaped paramecium with dense cilia carpet, large macronucleus, small micronucleus, oral groove indentation, and contractile vacuole.

cells.label: Amoeba
cells.summary: Shape-shifting protozoan that crawls using pseudopods and feeds by engulfing prey.
cells.funFact: An amoeba can change its entire body shape in seconds — a single cell doing amoeboid crawling is essentially a one-cell performance of macrophage behaviour.
facts: No fixed shape — cytoplasm flows into pseudopods by sol/gel transitions.
Engulfs prey by phagocytosis into food vacuoles.
Contractile vacuole pumps out excess water (freshwater osmoregulation).
Some species (Entamoeba histolytica, Naegleria fowleri) are pathogens.

components.label: Plasma membrane
components.fact: No cell wall; reshapes freely.

components.label: Nucleus
components.fact: Often centrally located, with a prominent nucleolus.

components.label: Pseudopods
components.fact: Actin polymerisation drives extension; myosin retracts.

components.label: Food vacuole
components.fact: Fuses with lysosomes for acidic digestion.

components.label: Contractile vacuole
components.fact: Characteristic of freshwater protists.

cad.prompt: Irregular amoeba with multiple finger-like pseudopods extending outward, central nucleus, food vacuole, and contractile vacuole.

cells.label: Ebola virus
cells.summary: Filamentous negative-sense RNA virus. Famous for its long, thread-like shape and high-lethality hemorrhagic fever.
cells.funFact: Ebola virions are among the longest viruses known — individual particles can stretch up to 14 µm, longer than many bacteria.
facts: Negative-sense single-stranded RNA genome (~19 kb).
GP surface glycoprotein binds host cell entry factors (NPC1 in endosome).
VP40 matrix protein drives filament budding from the plasma membrane.
Natural reservoir is likely fruit bats; outbreaks have high case fatality.

components.label: Lipid envelope
components.fact: Can bend and branch in native virions.

components.label: GP glycoprotein
components.fact: Target of approved monoclonal therapies (Inmazeb, Ebanga).

components.label: Helical nucleocapsid
components.fact: One of the longest contiguous RNA helices in the virosphere.

components.label: VP40 matrix
components.fact: Sufficient alone to produce virus-like particles.

lifecycle.label: Attachment
lifecycle.narration: GP binds host lectins (DC-SIGN, TIM-1) and is taken in by macropinocytosis.

lifecycle.label: Entry + uncoating
lifecycle.narration: Cathepsins cleave GP inside the endosome; GP binds NPC1; membranes fuse; RNA enters cytoplasm.

lifecycle.label: Replication
lifecycle.narration: Viral L polymerase transcribes genes and copies genome inside cytoplasmic inclusion bodies.

lifecycle.label: Assembly
lifecycle.narration: Nucleocapsid + VP40 + GP traffic to the plasma membrane and assemble into filaments.

lifecycle.label: Budding
lifecycle.narration: VP40 drives membrane curvature; new filamentous virions bud off and infect neighbouring cells.

cad.prompt: Filamentous Ebola virion — long curved envelope with GP spikes along the length, internal helical nucleocapsid, VP40 matrix caps.

cells.label: Mitochondrion
cells.summary: ATP powerhouse with cristae-folded inner membrane, matrix, and its own circular DNA.
cells.funFact: Mitochondria are the descendants of free-living α-proteobacteria absorbed ~1.5 billion years ago.
facts: Cristae enlarge the inner membrane surface area by ~5x.
ATP synthase sits on the cristae membrane.
Mitochondrial DNA inherits maternally.
Endosymbiotic origin from ancient α-proteobacteria.

components.label: Outer membrane
components.fact: Permeable to small molecules via porins.

components.label: Inner membrane
components.fact: Has 5x the surface area of the outer membrane.

components.label: Cristae
components.fact: ATP synthase rotates here at ~6,000 RPM.

components.label: Matrix
components.fact: Contains mitochondrial ribosomes, mtDNA, and the citric acid cycle.

components.label: mtDNA
components.fact: Encodes 13 proteins, 22 tRNAs, 2 rRNAs — the rest is imported.

cad.prompt: Stylised mitochondrion: ellipsoid outer + inner membrane, cristae folds, mtDNA loop.

cells.label: Ribosome
cells.summary: Ribonucleoprotein machine with large (60S) and small (40S) subunits translating mRNA.
cells.funFact: Ribosomes are the slowest known molecular machines that nonetheless out-produce nearly every other complex by mass.
facts: Peptidyl transferase activity lives in the rRNA, making it a ribozyme.
Eukaryotic 80S = 60S + 40S; prokaryotic 70S = 50S + 30S.
Bound to ER forms rough ER.
Polysomes = multiple ribosomes on one mRNA.

components.label: Large subunit (60S)
components.fact: Catalyses peptide bond formation via rRNA, not protein.

components.label: Small subunit (40S)
components.fact: Reads mRNA codons and selects the matching tRNA.

components.label: mRNA thread
components.fact: Multiple ribosomes can translate one mRNA at once (polysome).

cad.prompt: Stylised ribosome: large lumpy subunit, small subunit cap, mRNA tube through cleft.

cadPipeline.purpose: Convert each cell/organelle into parametric CAD via build123d (text-to-cad harness). Browser uses procedural Three.js geometry; build123d Python source is stored alongside for STEP/GLB regeneration when user installs build123d.
recommendedTools: text-to-cad skill pack (build123d + OCP) for STEP/STL/GLB export
rules: Each cell script defines a gen_step() returning build123d compound for named parts.
Browser never loads external GLB; geometry builder in assets/bio/geometry.js is the single runtime source.
